title: Introduction to Strudel
description: Learn live coding with Strudel patterns — from your first note to layered rhythms.

steps.title: Your first sound
steps.content:
# Your first sound

Strudel is a live coding environment for music. You write patterns in the editor and hear them loop in real time.

The editor already contains `note("c4")` — a middle C. Press **Play** to hear it.

steps.title: Change the pitch
steps.content:
# Change the pitch

Notes follow standard notation: `c4`, `d4`, `e4`, `f4`, `g4`, `a4`, `b4` — then `c5` an octave up.

Sharps use `#`: `c#4`, `f#4`. Flats use `b`: `bb4`, `eb4`.

Change `c4` to `e4` in the editor and press Play.

steps.title: A sequence
steps.content:
# A sequence

Separate notes with **spaces** to play them one after another, evenly spread across a cycle:

```
note("c4 e4 g4")
```

This plays C, E, G in sequence — a broken chord. Try it and press Play.

steps.title: Longer sequences
steps.content:
# Longer sequences

Add more notes to the sequence — Strudel fits them all into the same cycle length, so more notes = faster pace.

Try:
```
note("c4 d4 e4 f4 g4 a4 b4 c5")
```

Press Play to hear the scale.
steps.code: note("c4 d4 e4 f4 g4 a4 b4 c5")

steps.title: Chords
steps.content:
# Chords

Use **commas** to play notes simultaneously:

```
note("c4,e4,g4")
```

You can combine sequences and chords:
```
note("c4,e4,g4 d4,f4,a4")
```

Try the C and D minor chords alternating.

steps.title: Quiz: sequences vs chords
steps.content:
# Check your understanding

You've learned two ways to combine notes.

fitness.question: Which separator plays notes at the same time?
What happens when you add more notes to a sequence?
options: The notes play faster (same cycle length, more notes)

steps.title: Drum samples
steps.content:
# Drum samples

Use `s()` instead of `note()` to trigger samples. The most common are:

- `bd` — bass drum (kick)
- `sd` — snare drum
- `hh` — hi-hat
- `cp` — clap

Try a basic beat:
```
s("bd sd bd sd")
```

Press Play.

steps.title: Rests
steps.content:
# Rests

Use `~` to insert a rest (silence) in a pattern:

```
s("bd ~ sd ~")
```

This gives a classic kick-snare groove with space. Try varying the rest placement:
```
s("bd bd sd ~")
```

steps.title: Speed: fast and slow
steps.content:
# Speed

Chain `.fast(n)` to multiply the playback rate, or `.slow(n)` to divide it:

```
note("c4 e4 g4").fast(2)
```

Plays the pattern twice per cycle. `.slow(2)` stretches it over two cycles.

Add `.fast(2)` to your current pattern and hear the difference.

steps.title: Effects: reverb
steps.content:
# Effects

Strudel has built-in audio effects you chain with `.`:

- `.room(0.5)` — reverb (0 = dry, 1 = very wet)
- `.delay(0.3)` — echo
- `.lpf(800)` — low-pass filter (cuts highs)
- `.gain(0.5)` — volume (0–1)

Try:
```
note("c4 e4 g4 b4").room(0.8)
```

steps.title: Stacking patterns
steps.content:
# Stacking patterns

Use `stack()` to layer multiple patterns playing simultaneously:

```
stack(
  note("c4 e4 g4 e4"),
  s("bd ~ sd ~")
)
```

This combines a melodic pattern with a drum beat. Press Play.
steps.code:
stack(
  note("c4 e4 g4 e4"),
  s("bd ~ sd ~")
)

steps.title: Mini-notation: alternation
steps.content:
# Alternation with angle brackets

Angle brackets `< >` alternate between options on each cycle:

```
note("<c4 e4> g4")
```

The first position plays `c4` on cycle 1, `e4` on cycle 2, repeating. Great for subtle variation.

Try:
```
stack(
  note("<c4 e4 g4> <e4 g4>"),
  s("bd ~ sd ~")
)
```
steps.code:
stack(
  note("<c4 e4 g4> <e4 g4>"),
  s("bd ~ sd ~")
)

fitness.question: How do you add reverb to a pattern?
What does `stack()` do?
options: Saves the pattern to a file

steps.title: You're live coding!
steps.content:
# You're live coding!

You now know the fundamentals of Strudel:

- `note()` for pitched sounds, `s()` for samples
- **Spaces** for sequences, **commas** for chords, `~` for rests
- `.fast()` / `.slow()` for tempo
- `.room()`, `.delay()`, `.lpf()` for effects
- `stack()` to layer patterns
- `< >` for per-cycle alternation

The code below is a starting point — remix it, break it, make it yours.

```
stack(
  note("<c4 e4 g4> <d4 f4 a4>").room(0.4),
  s("bd ~ sd ~").fast(2),
  s("hh*4").gain(0.4)
)
```
steps.code:
stack(
  note("<c4 e4 g4> <d4 f4 a4>").room(0.4),
  s("bd ~ sd ~").fast(2),
  s("hh*4").gain(0.4)
)
